summary: I was born in Mogadishu, Somalia in 1989, but I only lived there for about a year before my family fled. Mogadishu was my first home, but the outbreak of the Somali Civil War in 1991 forced us to leave. Although I have few memories of Mogadishu itself, it remains an important part of my identity and my family's history.

The Somali Civil War broke out in 1991, and it changed my family's life forever. My parents made the difficult decision to leave Somalia because of the violence and instability caused by the war. This event led us to migrate to the United States, shaping my entire upbringing and sense of identity as a Somali in exile.

My family and I arrived in the United States shortly after fleeing Somalia in the early 1990s. We lived in several places including Kansas City, San Diego, and Minnesota. Growing up in the U.S. was a mix of opportunity and challenge; while life was good in many ways, I struggled with discrimination and questions about my identity as a Somali living here.

Kansas City, Missouri was the first place my family settled when we arrived in the United States. We stayed there for about a year before moving to San Diego. It was the beginning of our new life in America, though I was still very young and unaware of the bigger changes happening around us.

After leaving Kansas City, my family moved to San Diego, California in 1992. We lived there for several years before relocating to Minnesota. San Diego was part of my early childhood in the U.S., a place where my family tried to build stability after fleeing Somalia.

In 1999, my family moved to Mankato, Minnesota to be closer to relatives and friends. I spent part of my childhood there, and it was my first experience living in the Midwest. At that time, I didn't think much about our differences or why we were in the U.S., but Mankato was an important step in our journey.

After living in Mankato, my family moved to Eden Prairie, Minnesota, where there was a Somali neighborhood. It was there that I began to realize we were different and started to question my identity. Eden Prairie marked a turning point in understanding my Somali heritage and the immigrant experience in America.

I attended the University of Minnesota and graduated in 2011 with a B.A. in Sociology and African and African-American Studies. My studies there were deeply connected to my personal quest to understand Somali history and identity. The university provided me with the academic tools to explore the memories and experiences of Somalis like myself.

My father worked as a driver for the United States Embassy in Somalia before we left. He was very dedicated to his job and still holds on to his Somali driver's license as a reminder of that time. His role connected our family to the U.S. even before we migrated, and it influenced our journey to America.

Minneapolis became home to my family after we settled in Minnesota, especially as my mother started a successful Somali business in south Minneapolis. Many of my siblings graduated high school there and began their own families. Minneapolis represents a place of community and opportunity for us, even as I continue to navigate my identity as a Somali in exile.
